speaker: interviewer
text: My name is [? Raylene ?] [? Bresk. ?] I'm conducting an interview with Bluma Doman. Today is the 18th of February, 1996. The interview is being conducted in Johannesburg, South Africa, in English. Tape eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this picture is a picture of my parents' engagement. After the war, we knew that my mother had one sister living in [INAUDIBLE]. We wrote in-- my sister from Israel wrote a letter to the Jewish community. They gave her an answer that Auntie died in '42. But they-- they are two sons. And they gave the address. When my sister got the address, she wrote to them. And they sent back lots and lots of family pictures of my aunties. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my father as I remember him. He was a councilman in the city hall. And there was a big picture hanging in our house. But we didn't have the picture. But my sister found in Israel, a guy which was together with my father, councilman. And from that picture, my sister took out. And she made a-- a duplicate. He gave her the picture. And then she sent one to me, one to my brother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the house which I lived in Poland in [INAUDIBLE]. It doesn't look as nice as it was, because the building was new. And now, 50 years later, it's run down. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is the balcony where I was-- when I was tired in the summertime, I used to sleep on this balcony. We had a lounge chair. And I slept over there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the adjacent house. Because in Poland, the houses were stayed all together, no alleys. Wall to wall, they were all buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get these pictures from?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Poland, in Poland. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Recently?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to mention it, about the doctor. He was our family doctor before the war. His name was Dr. [INAUDIBLE]. He was a very, very good doctor. During the war, when Jewish people got sick, he went always to their rescue. Not even-- he didn't take-- no money for his visit. He left money for the medicine. He-- he was beaten up a few times from the Germans. Because the Germans wanted him to salute. And he said he would-- could never do it. After the war, I found out he was still-- still alive and only one son. He was a very, very good doctor, especially in the war to the Jews. This is a picture of Ozorkow ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there, we were tortured. And we didn't have enough food. But there were no elderly people. There were no children. In ghetto, just middle aged people were in and the youth. This is a picture of my late husband. He wasn't more than 24 years old. He looks like a 60 year old man. If he would be alive and we were making that video, he could tell you lots of horror stories. This is a picture of [INAUDIBLE], a death- a death camp also. Over there, they buried the children and their mothers alive. And now, I'm corresponding with a guy from Poland, like I told you previous. He went there. And he made those pictures for me. And he sent me the videos. The name of the Polack is [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE]. We became very good friends. I am corresponding with him and even sent him, last month, a video from all the family. He's very nice. He's intelligent. And he always helps the agency, which is doing the video for us in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a-- Hold on. Hold on

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Keep your hands [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the Lodz cemetery. I always cry that I don't know where my mother is buried. So my son tried to-- to a agency in Poland to locate. Maybe we can find her grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my son said, if we find the grave, I'm going to Poland. And I'm going to put down a headstone. But this never happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We talk to-- to the people in Lodz from the cemetery to have [INAUDIBLE]. They said, these are all the graves after the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prior to the last people leaving ghetto. So we have only the graves unmarked we can do. That's all we could do. This is a picture of me, the first pictures after the camps. I didn't have any teeth. All my teeth were broken from the camps, which that's why I keep my mouth closed. And after a while, I was-- was fit with dentures. I had only roots in my gum, the upper teeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your address in Bamberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We lived-- when this picture was taken, we lived on Martin Luther [INAUDIBLE] in Bamberg. And prior to that, we lived on [INAUDIBLE] in Bamberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What document is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first document we got after the war that the [INAUDIBLE] made us. [INAUDIBLE] because we didn't have any documents, nothing. This is a document after the concentration camp in Bamberg. The German authorities wanted to have something proved that we are really displaced persons. So we got this. It's written [NON-ENGLISH], concentration camp. And-- and we had to have a document that we were married. We came to Bamberg. We had to have a civil ceremony and get officially married. This is a picture of my late husband. It was given out to him by the United Nations right as we arrived in Bamberg. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] As you see, he's wearing a German jacket which the pilots had. It was a fur jacket. He loved that jacket. He always wore it in the winter time.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could say, my husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Ozorkow survivors. Once a year, once when we in Germany, we congregate in Landsberg. And we made our [INAUDIBLE] over there. These are Ozorkow-- all Ozorkow people. And this is written on the bottom. [INAUDIBLE] Oh, it is written on the bottom, 'remember what the [INAUDIBLE] did-- did to us.' And there is a stone. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Ozorkow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we congregated the first year, there with tears. Oh, you are alive, you are alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was nice to see people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Willie Fisher. You know, the toys which are in America? They're called the Fisher Toys. He was a Jewish soldier. And he met us in shul. And he adopted me and my husband. He used to come-- like I mentioned before-- he used to come every Friday night for the candle lighting. And he was thrilled. He always used to come in, open my cupboard, and see if I have enough food. He stays with a companion. They were playing in the American band. And he-- he always would say, you must come to see us play. His name was also Willie. And a lot of times he brought Willie with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What side was Willie Fisher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Willie Fisher is at the left side, the taller guy. And as you can see, my children also got [INAUDIBLE]. This is a picture of our documents that we've been married and all these where we lived. And this was really very helpful. It was a document they called [NON-ENGLISH]. We had to have over there some witnesses. And we had a civil performance with witnesses from our city that they knew us. And this was our document almost to the end of the-- to go to America. This is a document which we got from the Joint organization. And it tells us exactly where we were going in America. This was our document to show the authorities in America. After that, we got our green cards right in the beginning as we arrived to America. This is a document which we got from the Joint Distribution to come to America. It was a contact, which they had made that we are going to Rochester, New York. My husband is a weaver and he's going to get a job there. This is my granddaughter, which is not present with us. She is in Israel in school. She's named after my mother. I always call her mama, not by her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a tall lady. My granddaughter always asks me, from whom I am so tall? So I tell her that your grandmother was so tall, that you're named after her.

speaker: interviewee
text: All my children-- none of them-- my mother was left-handed. None of my children were left-handed. Only this granddaughter is left-handed, taken all from my grandmother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] great grandmother. This is my sister and brother. They live in Los Angeles. We are very close. We write to each other very often. And we talk over the phone. And they are very attached to me, because I was at home, the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell them to be quiet out there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoa, whoa, whoa, yeah. Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson, my daughter's. They live in America. She has an only son. He's now almost 14 year-- 14 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He looks just like his mama. This is a picture of me and my late husband. There was-- it was a good time, because we went to New York to his wedding. They were one of our most precious moments to bring our son to the chuppah. And he was married in New York on the steps of the Yeshiva. The-- the ceremony got so long, a few rabbis gave them the blessings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoa, whoa.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a tallis and Seder and tefillin, which my husband got as soon as we arrived to Bamberg. And the Joint distributed everything. And I kept it for a memory. In the Seder, every time a grandchild was born, he put in the Jewish dates of the birthdays. The little case for the tallis, I made it. He said there has to be a-- so I-- we bought some velvet in Bamberg. And I made him that tallis, which I still keep it and treasure it. Because my husband, as you see, my husband was a religious man. He loved Yiddishkeit. And so did I. This is candlesticks, which I got in Bamberg from a German lady. She said, after the war, we used to go to them. They been selling us a lot of things. This, she said, you take it. It's for you. I treasured this all the years. And when my son got married in New York, we brought for him this candlestick. It's pure-- it's silver. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was lighting Shabbas candles for years in it. But when we went to my son's wedding, I said to my husband, we're going to take this along and give it to Mosche. And Mosche treasures it till now. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My daughter-in-law lights candlesticks for many, many years in this candelabra. [VIDEO PLAYBACK] - A note was found in a wagon after it was returned to the ghetto. It said, the deportees were approaching [NON-ENGLISH], the slaughterhouse. If a ghetto man ever gained power over the world, he wouldn't hesitate a moment before having it destroyed. And he would be doing the one right thing. - August 28, 1944. From the window of an attic hiding place, we can see the masses moving on [INAUDIBLE] Street. The last transport is to leave on Tuesday. Anyone who stays in the ghetto beyond that date will face capital punishment. This notice has frightened almost everyone who was planning to hide with us here. They have decided to report. They admire our courage. But they doubt whether we can survive here and think we'll follow after them at the last moment. Everyone wants to be first. No one wants to be last onto those trains. [END PLAYBACK]
